.@RhysLindmark's post-capitalism article is well-written and beautifully illustrated.

Something struck me though.

Status is *not* zero-sum as Rhys claims. It can be positive-sum—like money—and even in ways better than money.

Thread 👇

https://t.co/VtaG5qabpj

Status is actually a lot closer to wealth than it seems.

- you can overflow it to others if you have enough yourself, just like money
- you can create it through win-wins (aka gains from trade)
- you can even create whole new status ladders

First, you can overflow status to others by e.g.

- promoting their work
- publicly supporting them
- championing an important, unpopular cause

If you're high-status, that's a small "status spend" for you and a big boost for them, so it's net positive.

Another positive-sum status move is making a useful introduction or cosigning someone (h/t @eriktorenberg).

They gain from the connection, and you gain by association. Gains from trade for status.

That's not just net-positive. That's a win-win-win.

Finally, every community has its own status hierarchy.

Want to create status? Start a new community.

Way easier than creating new money (crypto helps, but that's still a long way from mainstream)

So, "status as wealth" is a valid analogy. Both are positive-sum resources.

I do agree that the mindset shift we need is to focus on the right kind of status.

We can choose to grant status for generosity and expanding awareness away from our present self.

@RhysLindmark What makes Ultimate special is of course spirit. 

The reason it's special is that it solves Goodhart's problem: when a metric (the score) becomes a target (how we define success), it stops being a good metric (maximizing score at the expense of joy is not success).

@RhysLindmark A solution to Goodhart's problem is pairing a quantitative metric with a quality metric.

Followers + level of discussion

GDP + life satisfaction

Score + spirit
@RhysLindmark The most respected teams in ultimate score high on wins and on spirit (e.g. Revolver, Fury, GoP 😉). But spirit is not very legible. So mainstream sports don't emphasize it.

@RhysLindmark As ultimate becomes more like mainstream sports, it risks losing that special sauce. 

Growth and spirit aren't inherently exclusive, but it takes a special effort to have both.

I've been coding for two decades now, mostly for profit and always for fun. 

Here are the rules I've developed over the years. 

Source: primarily web dev and systems programming on complex projects that I've maintained over many years and many rewrites. 

🧵

1. code is debt

users don't care about the code. every line of code has costs (your time, readability, maintainability, complexity)

its like owning a house with lots of rooms. its nice when your friends come over once a month, but you pay rent every day.

if you're pedantic, an unhedged call option is a better analogy

https://t.co/WGmIDNOf6R

2. DRY DRY DRY

A place for everything, and everything in it's place.

3. keep it simple (especially at first)

Complex working systems grow from simple working systems. If you find yourself with something complex that's not working, you're in for a lot of pain.

More on how to do this:

https://t.co/bXXIPvl7eC

4. be consistent. be predictable. no magic.

These reduce cognitive load. Be kind to future-you, who will forget absolutely everything you know now about your code. Make the code obvious.

Save the magic to delight your users.

5. no magic numbers/strings — use constants instead

No:  admins := getUsers("admin")
Yes: PERM_ADMIN = "admin"; admins := getUsers(PERM_ADMIN)

It's prone to breaking and kills IDE's usage finder.

Strings are for input/output. Everywhere else, use a constant.

6. a good repro is 90% of a bugfix

Start fixing every bug by first reproducing it. Usually that's enough to know what to do. If not, delete code till nothing but the repro is left. It also helps to reduce the cycle time through the "make a change and try it" loop.

7. code is written once, but is read many times

You're the worst judge of your own code at the moment you're writing it. If anyone tells you it's confusing, then it's confusing. Optimize for readability and remember future-you:

https://t.co/SKgNUZjrW7

8. ship early, ship often

Everyone's on board with lean/agile these days, so I shouldn't need to convince you on this one. Give yourself deadlines and don't miss them (cut scope if you must).

9. first code, then ship, then measure, and only then optimize

Speed is a feature, but you're paying for it with time and complexity. Only buy speed once you *know* it's lacking.

10. remember the 80/20 rule, and the 90/90 rule

Dont get stuck on the little things, they'll kill your productivity. But thats no excuse to pile hacks on hacks forever. Budget time for cleaning up the messes you'll no doubt make.

https://t.co/WgU5wUxZts

https://t.co/y3bvGfyK4t

🏋️why lift?

the ROI for lifting is huge, higher than just about anything else I do. main benefits: 

- injury prevention
- long-term health
- confidence loops (i count at least 3)
- it's just fun
- excellence is a virtue

🧵

my top reason to lift is i get hurt less. injuries are the WORST!

i love to play sports (mostly ultimate, lately volleyball), climb stuff, roughhouse with my kids, just do physical things

the stronger i am, the less hurt i get. simple as that

more generally, lifting promotes long-term health. physical, mental, emotional... all of it (you're a whole person, not a collection of parts)

our bodies are antifragile. they need to be challenged. a lifting habit gets you that challenge efficiently and sustainably.

its hard to get into regular lifting (habits are hard) but once you're in, the benefits compound. 

i call these "confidence loops":

- accomplishment (do hard things)
- aesthetics (lo and feel good)
- capableness (capacity to do things with your body)

accomplishment loop

set lifting goals → hit them → feel good → confidence increases → build evidence of yourself as someone who does hard things → set higher goals

caveat: set the right goals, or better yet use a system. try Starting Strength or Stronglifts to start

aesthetics loop

lift weights → look attractive → positive feedback from others → feel good → confidence increases → lift more

https://t.co/e0dICGzZUx

capableness loop

lift → get better at sports → you like things more when you're good at them, and others like you more when you're good at stuff → happiness and confidence increase → lift more

bonus: sports is great for community, meeting people, etc

all three are valid and useful places to start. pick the one that resonates with you.

the capableness loop is the one that first hooked me when i got serious about ultimate in college

beyond all that, lifting is just fun (at least for me). 

at work i'm constantly swimming in uncertainty. success is hard to define and harder to achieve.

lifting is the opposite: clear goals, clear sense of progress. did you lift today? if yes, you win!

finally, excellence is a virtue in itself. its good to be good at things. lifting is a thing you can be good at. it's fully within your control. conveniently, it has all these other great benefits too.

@sarelbic Happy to chat about kids. I have 2

me, reading management advice: gonna try this with my kids!

me, reading parenting advice: gonna try this at work!

the overlap is at least 80%

a core parenting idea: "don't make it a thing"

don't create points of repeated conflict with your kids

you're creative! there's almost always a win-win if you look hard enough

an example: hitting

at a recent daycare meeting, parents complained that no matter what they try, their kids like to hit them and others. 

suggested solutions were banal: tell the kid its wrong, punish them, even restrain them until they stop!

thats how hitting becomes a thing

instead, my first step is always "maybe let them?"

if you don't feel strongly (not a dealbreaker for you, not against family values, etc), try to say "Yes"

 for hitting that doesn't work for me, so on to idea 2...

if you don't want to say just "yes", you could try "yes, and here's how". teach your kids how to do what they want in a way you're ok with

back at the daycare meeting:

me: we let my daughter hit if she follows our two rules
everyone else: 🤯

our rules? 
1. don't hurt anyone
2. first get permission

how that works:

3yo: "can i hit you?"
me: "here's my arm. please be gentle"

or

me: "where?"
3yo: "in the face"
me: "you know i don't like that. how about over here?"

or

me: "not right now"

she listens

that's just one example. the point is to empathize and get creative. redirect them. figure out what they really want, and how to meet their needs. be on the same team.

then, when you need to pull out the emphatic "no", it's that much more effective

Would you want to live in a world where, instead of being considered an adult when you turn an age (automatic adulthood), you become an adult by explicitly declaring that you are one (opt-in adulthood)?

🧵 to brainstorm some implications

what do I mean by "adult"?

i mean someone who can make legally-binding decisions, and who society views as independent from their parents/guardians.

if you invite an adult on a trip to a foreign country, you don't expect to say "i'll need to ask my mom"

by opting into adulthood, you are no longer a child

you declare to the world that you don't want others to see or treat you as one anymore

and you get all the rights, and all the responsibilities, that come with that:

you can now enter into contracts, vote, be employed, pay taxes, serve on a jury, go to jail, take out loans, enlist, be responsible for children (including your own), consent to sex, own property and money, consume restricted substances, run for office, etc

note that in this world, you can opt into adulthood at ANY time: when you're 10, when you're 20, when you're 50

whenever you perform the ceremony to signal your intent, POOF! you're an adult

until you do, you are a child
one consequence: presumably adults would wear a sign or special clothing, since you can no longer infer adulthood based on age
another: you would not make this decision in a vacuum. everyone you know would be aware that this is how things work. you'd personally know lots of ppl who have already opted in, and lots who haven't

there would be social technology to help you make sense of this. you'd take adulting classes in school, discuss with friends, browse Peter Pan forums, watch movies about the kid who rage-opted at age 5, etc

upsides:

biological age is only a rough proxy for adult-readiness. this option is more inclusive of personal preference

some people are not yet ready for independence at 18 (this is one function of college) or even at 25. others are ready at 14.

upsides:

the right to exit is vital to any healthy institution. inability to exit = monopoly. 

some people are stuck in awful relationships with their parents. maybe you're gay and your family is violently intolerant. now you have the option for a way out.

downsides:

- there's no going back. in the language of @trexproject, opting into adulthood would be a transformative experience. it would change you as a person and you won't fully know what it's like until you do it.

but maybe that's not such a bad thing

downsides:

- a new avenue for coercion. predatory lending to 6-year-olds ("opt-in, sign here, own this pony!").

- less pressure to make family life pleasant ("don't like 8pm curfew? you can always opt out")

- some will choose poorly, regret it, and suffer the consequences

but, keep in mind 2nd-order effects. you won't be in a vacuum. society will support you. 

so, would you want to live in such a world?

fin. for now.

short 🧵on the planning fallacy, or why I won't hit 50 threads by end of year

when you think about how long something will take, your instinct is to imagine the *best case scenario* for *you*

50 threads ... 50 days ... one a day is easy!

that's called the inside view. its inside your head, inside your current situation

instead, start with the base rate: how many threads does a typical person write? 

it's far less than one a day

then make GENTLE adjustments for your situation, focusing especially on how things could go wrong

a startup, 2 young kids, little time spent on twitter ... all reasons to think it will take longer or won't happen at all

this is my 6th thread in 10 days. 2x slower than planned

also, estimates are fat-tailed

you can take WAY more time than you planned, but not way less time

when's the last time your flight was 6 hours early? what about 6 hours late?

finally, remember Hofstadter's law:

it always takes longer than you expect, even when you take into account Hofstadter's Law.

summary of this paper on the top 10 strongest findings in behavioral genetics 

strap in, its fascinating stuff

but first, a definition you need to know...

https://t.co/rqedobyybK

a crucial word in🧬context is heritability: the degree of outward variation of a trait in a group that is due to gene variation between ppl in the group

❌: how much of *my* height comes from *my* genes
✅: how much do *differences* in genes influence *diffs* in height btwn ppl

1. All psychological traits show genetic influence

as a rule of thumb, psych traits are about 30-50% heritable

- iq: ~50%
- personality (whatever that means): 30-50%
- well-being: 36%

compare with a physical trait like height, which is 90% heritable

2. No traits are 100% heritable

seem obvious, but it provides the strongest evidence for the importance of environmental influence after controlling for genetic influence

so always control for genetic influence when investigating environmental influence

3. Heritability is caused by many genes of small effect

there's not "gene for X" in behavioral traits

this will be important in findings 9 and 10

4. Phenotypic correlations between psychological traits are mainly due to genetics

~60% of correlation between iq, reading, math, and language test scores is explained by genetic variation

5. The heritability of intelligence increases throughout development 

this is surprising. you'd expect that as people age, environment explains more of the diff between them

but the opposite is true. the older people are, the greater the heritability of iq

heritability stats:

age 9: 40%
12: 55%
17: 65%
may get as high as 80% later in life

other domains like personality do not show systematic changes in heritability during development

why? it could be "innovation" (new genetic influences coming online as you develop). or could be the next finding...

6. The stability of traits as you age is mainly due to genetics. Changes to your traits as you age are mainly due to environment

so how can the heritability of intelligence increase with age if genetic effects are stable?

one explanation could be "genetic amplification": small genetic differences are amplified as kids choose, modify, and create environments correlated with their genetic propensities

wait, did I just say that genes affect your environment? well ...

7. Most measures of the “environment” show significant genetic influence

when separating nature vs nurture, if you're not careful, most of the things under "nurture" will be influenced by nature

this effect shows up in many domains: parenting, neighborhood, school, work, etc

example: the risk of mental illness is higher in cities vs rural areas (ostensibly a comparison of environments)

but your choice to live in a big city vs small town is itself influenced by your genes

https://t.co/kGdr63senx
another example: in studies of twin children, parenting reflects genetic differences in children’s characteristics (personality, psychopathology)

8. Most associations between environmental measures and psychological traits are genetically mediated 

no surprise by now: Everything is Heritable™️

e.g don't assume that correlations between parenting and children’s behavior is due to the env effect of parenting on behavior...

... Could be that genetic factors affect both parenting and children’s behavior

theres a solid correlation between family socioeconomic status (env) and children's IQ (psysch trait). But GCTA shows that the genes account for 94% of that correlation and same genes affecting both

9. Most environmental effects are not shared btwn children growing up in the same family

you'd think that kids in the same family would be similar

genes account for most of that similarity

while no traits are 100% heritable, env doesn't contribute much to the similarity

this is not saying that family experience is unimportant. only that the relevant experiences are specific to each kid.

could be a case of "many experiences of small effect", analogous to finding 3

10. Abnormal is normal

traits are normally distributed. common psych disorders are the extremes of the same genetic factors responsible for heritability throughout the distribution

implication of that: disorders are the negative tail of the curve, but there's also the positive tail

this points to an opportunity for "positive genetics" resilience rather than vulnerability; how children flourish rather than flounder

@michaelcurzi @generativist @BecomingCritter Here’s how it works:

- set aside $X
- ask ppl to submit grants for funding
- once grants are in, ask ppl to give their own money to grants
- each donation gets matched from the pool, using a clever formula based on how many diff ppl contributed to it and how much they gave total

@michaelcurzi @generativist @BecomingCritter In this model you’re not picking what’s cool on your own. Everyone picks together. Ends up building community better and surfaces stuff you may not like but others do.

I've stopped seeing food items as Platonic ideals and started seeing them as an end result of a long and idiosyncratic process of combining and processing

its totally changed how I think about healthy eating

think about putting together an ikea desk. the wood comes from a lumber mill and is cut into pieces in one place. the metal for the screws is mined and forged in other places. the rubber for the little feet is grown on a tree... this is called a supply chain

food is the same way. plants suck in nutrients from the sun and soil and water and air. whatever you put on or around them, ends up in them. they are picked, shaped, processed, and eventually get eaten

animals are further downstream. they eat these plants, absorb and process them with their bodies, then eventually produce meet and milk and so on. they also eat antibiotics, run around, get sick.

all this stuff eventually makes its way to you

its like katamari. everything you roll up becomes a part of you. and you're rolling up stuff that is also rolling up other stuff around it.

so its not "milk", its a chain of rolled-up and processed stuff

then ask: how much of all that stuff is stuff you really want inside you?
that's my mental model for eating healthy: how good is your nutrition supply chain?

my daughter's 5th birthday is this week

if i had to summarize my high-level take on childhood development in a few sentences, id say ...

everything kids do is their best attempt to meet the challenges of life. when they act in a way you don't like, they're just solving the problem of "i feel bad" or "im not getting what i want" as best as they know how.
they're mostly not in direct control of this, though they can learn better ways to solve their problems over time and with practice.
our job as parents is to love them unconditionally, guide them in their growth, and not put too much of our own shit on them.

btw adults are no different. we're bigger kids with more physical growth and experience and baggage of things we learned and "learned" over our life. but we're still doing the same things -- trying to meet our needs in the ways we think best

short thread on essential qualities in great leaders. there are only two imho

1. self-awareness

"know thyself" said the Greeks, and they were right 

the hardest part of leadership is managing your psychology (h/t @bhorowitz), and self-awareness is the key to that. in fact self-awareness solves most problems

you can generally find people to do things you're not great at or hate doing. but no one can do your self-awareness-ing for you

though you _can_ get a coach to help you develop self-awareness. and you should! im very bullish on coaching

2. wholeheartedness

embodying your values in everything you do. a unity of conscience and will. actions and desires in constant alignment with your beliefs.

...which implies having strong values, so maybe this one's really two things (and values come from self-awareness 😉)

everything else -- vision, courage, drive, clarity, inspiration -- is downstream of these qualities

if you max them, people will want to follow you and help you achieve your goals

not saying it'll be easy

still takes time/work to find those ppl, communicate your vision, resolve coordination problems and other roadblocks, etc etc etc. even getting to a place of strong self-awareness and wholehearted commitment can take a while.

but without em you're lost

what's the deal with fractalism? here's the deal

i'm not a fan of labeling myself or declaring allegiance to a belief system. but its convenient to have a concise word for how i see things. so i decided to just make one up that fits me!

welcome to fractalism https://t.co/EnubjyNKAM

fractals are shapes that have reveal more and more detail as you zoom in on parts of them. they are often self-similar at different scales, you can zoom forever (there's no bottom), and they tend to look fun on acid

the core idea of fractalism is that reality is fractal

look around you. everywhere you see things that, if you imagine zooming in on them, will reveal to you more their complexity

this is most obvious in nature -- the veins of a leaf🍃resembles the branches of a tree🌲

rocks feel smooth when you touch them but look like mountain landscapes with a microscope. clouds, shell spirals, shorelines, ant colonies, blood vessels, snowflakes, road maps

are all examples of fractals https://t.co/HBTK1PNtHe

but its not just things. knowledge is fractal too. the more you pay attention to something and learn about it, the more detail you discover.

zoomed out: my finger hurts
zoomed in: neurons sending signals
more zoomed: neurotransmitters cascades
even more zoomed: ...

@tasshinfogleman told me that since he started learning to draw faces, he notices a lot more detail in someone's face -- thin eyebrows, sharp nose, big forehead, etc etc. the more you look, the more there is to see. https://t.co/cLbZR26ER9

an idea can be summarized in one sentence, or one tweet, or thread, or essay, or book. and even reading the 100th biography of Winston Churchill, you'll discover new detail you didn't know before.

as they say, the map is not the territory

(this is prolly a good time to mention that im not an expert in any of this stuff. there's prolly a whole field of "fractal studies" that i know nothing about, so its all rough and handwavey and im often talking out my ass. feels true to me though, and im gonna keep zooming in😜) https://t.co/QSVIccZOS5

RT @RichDecibels: guy who has never hosted a dinner party: I should start a network state
"its an experiment" are the magic words you need to quiet your ego so you can do wildly new things without fear of ridicule or failure

when you do something new, you're gonna suck at it for a while. that hurts in itself, but the pain gets way worse when you imagine others seeing you sucking

what do?
reframe: you're doing an experiment to see how this new thing goes

why does that work?

a) science is high-status. experimentation is cool (that's what college is for, right?). you're not attached to any outcome. there's no failure as long as you're learning.

b) you've created some distance between "you" and "the experiment". your ego is safe. if it doesn't work out, your core identity as person who's good at stuff remains unstained

now you can't mess up

actually that's not true. you've bought freedom from your inner and outer critics, but you *can* still blow it by not actually learning anything.

get complacent, don't commit to the bit, lose accountability ... and you've cheated yourself

so experiment wholeheartedly
